content.text: Deploying Claude across an organisation is not a single purchase decision — it is a multi-layer system of access controls, workflow configuration, individual adoption, and (eventually) programmatic integration. 'Claude in the Org' refers to the full picture: how IT, department leaders, individual contributors, and engineering teams each interact with Claude at their own layer, and how those layers fit together into a coherent organisational capability.

Getting this right matters because the most common failure mode in enterprise AI deployment is not technical — it is structural. Teams buy seats, give everyone access, and then watch usage stagnate because nobody owns the configuration, the shared Projects are empty, and individual members don't know what they're allowed to use Claude for.

attrs.alt: Claude deployment layers: Access and Identity, Workspace Configuration, Daily Workflows, and Integration and Automation

content.text: The four-layer model above describes the responsibilities at each level. Each layer can restrict but not expand what the layer above permits. Engineering cannot configure API access if IT has not negotiated it with Anthropic. Individual members cannot use models that the workspace admin has restricted.
Layer 1: Access and Identity (IT and Security)
IT's job is to answer three questions before anyone else can do anything: who is allowed to use Claude, under what data-handling terms, and how do we know when someone's access should change.
Decisions at This Layer
Plan selection:
Team or Enterprise. Enterprise is required if IT mandates SSO for all SaaS. Team is sufficient if manual seat management is acceptable.
Data residency:
Which region processes conversation data? Default is US. EU and APAC options require Enterprise. Decision is driven by legal/compliance requirements, not preference.
Acceptable-use policy:
What may employees use Claude for? What data may they share with it? The policy should exist in writing before seats are distributed. Without it, employees make their own risk judgements.
SSO and SCIM:
How are accounts provisioned and deprovisioned? SCIM eliminates orphan seats (ex-employees with active access). Without SCIM, IT must manually remove members on offboarding.
Audit log routing:
Where does the audit log go? If the security team needs to query it, it should be routed to the SIEM (Splunk, Datadog, Elastic) not just left in the Admin Console export.
Common IT Mistakes
Deploying without an acceptable-use policy. Employees default to using Claude for whatever seems useful, including tasks that involve customer data or regulated information.
Skipping SCIM because 'the team is small'. This defers a known risk — every employee who leaves and retains Claude access is a security liability. Configure SCIM at initial deployment.

Not testing SSO before enabling enforcement. If SSO breaks after enforcement is turned on, no members can log in until the issue is resolved. Test with 5 users and confirm recovery path before enforcing org-wide.
Layer 2: Workspace Configuration (Department Heads and AI Leads)

Once IT has provisioned access, the second layer is how teams are organised and how Claude is configured for their specific workflows. This layer belongs to department heads, team leads, or designated 'AI leads' — people who understand the team's workflows well enough to write useful custom instructions and curate knowledge files.
Workspace Taxonomy
Enterprise organisations should map their workspace structure to their org chart or workflow map, not create a single global workspace. A flat single-workspace structure means:
One instruction tries to serve all use cases (support, engineering, legal, marketing) — it ends up too generic to be useful.
All members can see all Shared Projects, including those belonging to other teams.
No way to apply model restrictions to specific teams (e.g. limiting the customer data team to lower-context models for cost and compliance reasons).
A well-designed workspace structure for a 200-person company might look like:
Workspace
Members
Model policy
Example Projects
Engineering
All engineers
All models
Code Review, Architecture Q&A, PR Summariser
Customer Support
All support agents
Sonnet + Haiku only
Tier 1 Triage, Escalation Drafts, Knowledge Base Q&A
Legal
Legal team + select advisors
Sonnet + Opus
Contract Review, Regulatory Research, Policy Drafts
Marketing
Marketing + design
All models
Campaign Copy, SEO Research, Brand Voice Check
Executive
Leadership team
All models
Board Prep, Strategic Analysis, Competitive Intel
Designating an AI Lead per Team

The single highest-leverage investment a department can make is designating one person as the team's AI lead — the workspace admin who owns the Shared Projects, writes and maintains custom instructions, curates knowledge files, and acts as the internal expert for that team's Claude usage.

The AI lead does not need to be a technical person. They need to understand the team's workflows deeply enough to write a good custom instruction, and have the authority to make the Projects the team's default workflow rather than an optional tool.
Time commitment: typically 2–4 hours of setup, then 1–2 hours per month to update knowledge files and review how the Project is being used.

Key skills: ability to write clear, concise process instructions; understanding of what information Claude should and should not reference; willingness to test the Project and iterate on the instruction.
Success metric: team members start conversations in the Shared Project by default rather than in personal sessions. When this happens, knowledge is shared and the organisation benefits.
Layer 3: Daily Workflows (Individual Contributors)

Individual contributors interact with Claude at the conversation level. Their relationship with Claude in an organisational context is shaped by two things: the Projects that have been set up for them (Layer 2), and the personal habits they develop for their own work.
When to Use a Shared Project vs Personal Session
Situation
Use Shared Project
Use Personal Session
Answering a customer support question using company policy
Yes — policy is in knowledge files, instruction maintains tone
No — inconsistency risk
Reviewing code against team standards
Yes — standards in knowledge files
Only if reviewing personal side project
Drafting a report about a customer (contains PII)
Depends on Project's data policy
Safer if data handling in Shared Project is not confirmed
Learning a new skill or exploring a topic personally
No — personal learning is not team work
Yes
Creating a reusable template or script the team will use
Yes — save as Artifact for team access
No — team won't see it
Responsible Use in an Organisational Context
Individual contributors need to understand the practical obligations of using Claude in a work context, beyond what the acceptable-use policy formally requires:
Attribution:
If a document will be presented externally or published, disclose whether AI was used in drafting it. Company policies and industry norms on AI disclosure vary — follow your organisation's policy.
Verification:

Claude can confidently produce plausible-but-incorrect information. Always verify factual claims against authoritative sources before using them in decisions, client deliverables, or regulatory filings.
Data handling:
Do not paste customer PII, trade secrets, or classified information into Claude unless your organisation's acceptable-use policy and the plan's data handling terms explicitly permit it.
Output ownership:

AI-generated content is typically not copyright-protected in the same way as human-authored work. Consult your legal team if output will be submitted for copyright protection or used in IP-sensitive contexts.
Adoption Lifecycle

attrs.alt: Four-phase organisational adoption curve from Pilot to Integration

content.text: Successful enterprise deployments follow a recognisable pattern. Skipping phases — particularly skipping the structured pilot in favour of an immediate org-wide rollout — is a common mistake that leads to low adoption and wasted seats.
Phase 1: Pilot

Run a 4–8 week pilot with 5–25 volunteers from one or two teams. The goal is not to generate ROI — it is to learn: which use cases are actually useful, what the right Project configuration looks like, what questions employees have, and what guardrails are needed. Document these findings before expanding.
Phase 2: Department Rollout

Expand to 3–5 teams with dedicated workspace admins (AI leads) in each. Configure Shared Projects based on pilot learnings. Run a 30-minute onboarding session per team that explains: what Claude is for in this team's context, what the Project instructions are, and what data they should and should not share. Passive 'just give everyone access' rollouts have low adoption.
Phase 3: Org-Wide

At 100+ seats, operational maturity matters as much as individual use. This means: a documented AI policy, an active AI lead in each department, a regular review of knowledge file freshness, a feedback channel for members to report problems or successes, and a process for adding new Shared Projects when new workflows emerge.
Phase 4: Integration

Engineering-built integrations — Claude in CI/CD pipelines, internal tools, Slack bots, customer-facing automations — are where AI compounds into measurable productivity. This phase requires the Anthropic API (separate from the claude.ai subscription), engineering ownership, and human-in-the-loop gates for high-stakes automated decisions.
AI Policy: What It Should Cover

An organisational AI policy is the document that answers employee questions before they make bad decisions. It should be written before org-wide deployment, kept short enough to actually be read, and reviewed annually.
Policy section
What it should say
Permitted uses
What work tasks Claude may be used for. Be specific: 'drafting internal communications', 'code review', 'summarising meeting notes'. Avoid vague 'anything work-related'.
Data restrictions
What data employees must not share with Claude. Typically: customer PII, trade secrets, draft M&A materials, regulated data (HIPAA, PCI) unless the plan's terms permit it.
Output review requirement
Which AI outputs require human review before use. High-stakes outputs (client deliverables, regulatory filings, code deployed to production) should always be reviewed by a human.
Attribution
When to disclose that AI was used in creating a document or decision. Match your industry's norms and any client contractual requirements.
Incident reporting
What to do if Claude produces harmful, biased, or confidentiality-violating output. Who to report to, and how.
Acceptable model use
Which models employees may use for which tasks. If cost control is important, specify that high-context or expensive models require manager approval for extended use.
Measuring Adoption and Value

One of the hardest problems in enterprise AI deployment is demonstrating value to leadership. Seat utilisation numbers ('73% of seats were active this week') are a poor proxy for value. A seat that runs 20 low-value conversations is less valuable than one that runs 3 high-value ones. Better measurement frameworks focus on outcomes, not activity.
Metrics That Indicate Real Adoption
Metric
What it measures
How to track it
Tasks shifted to Claude
Specific task types that members have stopped doing manually
Team survey at 30/60/90 days post-rollout
Artifact reuse rate
Artifacts created in Shared Projects and referenced by other members
Project admin counts artifacts + asks team
Knowledge file reference rate
Whether Claude is actually using knowledge files or generating from memory
Test conversations that require file content; spot-check responses
Time-to-first-draft reduction
Subjective measure: how long does a task take now vs before Claude
Team survey; compare output timestamps if feasible
Conversation-to-output ratio
Conversations that produce a saved Artifact or forwarded output vs those abandoned
Qualitative review of Project history

Avoid measuring seat utilisation or message counts as success metrics. These measure activity, not value. A team that uses Claude for one high-value use case consistently is more successful than one with high seat utilisation scattered across low-value tasks.
Running a Value Review at 90 Days
At the 90-day mark of a department rollout, the AI lead should run a structured value review. This is a 30-minute team meeting with a structured agenda:
What are the 3 tasks team members use Claude for most frequently? Are these the highest-value uses, or did the team default to low-complexity tasks?
What tasks have been identified as good Claude use cases but are not yet in a Shared Project? These are the next Projects to create.
What has gone wrong? Where has Claude produced incorrect information, required heavy correction, or produced outputs the team could not use?
Is the custom instruction in each Project still accurate? Has the team's workflow changed since the instruction was written?
Are knowledge files current? Which files have become stale and need updating?
Cross-Team Knowledge Sharing

One underused capability in organisations with multiple workspaces is cross-team knowledge sharing. A customer support team may have discovered an effective instruction pattern for handling complaints. An engineering team may have built a code review Project that the data science team could adapt. Without a mechanism for sharing these configurations, every team reinvents the same wheel.
Building an Internal AI Playbook

An internal AI playbook is a living document that captures the organisation's best Project configurations, most effective instructions, and lessons learned. It is not a policy document — it is a practical guide written by the AI leads for other AI leads.
Custom instruction templates: shareable starting points for common Project types (support, code review, document drafting, research). Each template includes example usage and known limitations.
Knowledge file standards: which file formats work best, how to structure reference documents for Claude readability, how to name files consistently across Projects.
Use case catalogue: a list of tasks the organisation has found Claude reliably good at, tasks where outputs require heavy review, and tasks to avoid entirely.
Failure mode log: cases where Claude produced incorrect or problematic outputs and how the team responded. This helps other teams avoid the same mistakes.

The playbook should live in a Shared Project itself — where it is indexed, searchable, and accessible to all AI leads. The irony of using Claude to help maintain the playbook about using Claude is not lost, and it is also genuinely useful: new AI leads can ask Claude questions about the playbook's guidance.
Coordinating AI Leads Across Departments

In organisations with 5+ departments, a monthly AI leads sync meeting is worth the cost. This 30-minute meeting covers: new Projects launched this month, failures and lessons learned, upcoming workflow changes that will require instruction updates, and proposed changes to the AI policy. The meeting should have a rotating facilitator and a shared note document.
Governance and Risk Management

As Claude usage matures in an organisation, governance becomes less about preventing bad use and more about maintaining quality, consistency, and compliance over time. The risks in a mature deployment are different from those in a pilot.
Risk Profile by Deployment Phase
Phase
Primary risks
Governance response
Pilot
No policy; employees experiment with sensitive data; no controls on what gets shared
Written acceptable-use policy; explicit data restrictions; designated AI lead
Department
Stale instructions; outdated knowledge files; members not using Projects consistently
Quarterly Project review; AI lead accountability; onboarding for new members
Org-wide
Orphan seats (ex-employees); policy drift (policy written but not followed); cross-team inconsistency
SCIM deprovisioning; annual policy review; AI leads coordination meeting
Integration
Automated outputs without human review; API key leakage; cost overruns; model behaviour changes in production
Output validation gates; API key rotation; cost alerts; staged model upgrades
Handling AI Output Incidents

An AI output incident is any case where Claude produced output that caused harm or near-miss harm: incorrect information used in a client deliverable, a draft email that should not have been sent, or a code suggestion with a security vulnerability. Organisations should have a lightweight incident response process:
Report: member reports the incident to the AI lead. AI lead records: what was the prompt, what was the output, what was the impact or near-miss.
Contain: if the output was shared externally or used in a deliverable, notify the affected parties and correct the record.
Investigate: was this a one-off (model hallucination on an unusual question) or a systemic problem (the instruction or knowledge file actively misled Claude)?
Update: if systemic, update the instruction or knowledge file. Add the failure mode to the internal playbook. If one-off, document it and move on.
Review: if the incident involved regulated data or external harm, escalate to Legal and update the AI policy if the acceptable-use rules need tightening.

Documentation principle: incident records should be factual and specific — what prompt, what output, what happened, what was fixed. Vague records ('Claude gave wrong information once') do not improve the system.
Integrating Claude with Existing Tools
Beyond the claude.ai interface, mature deployments integrate Claude output into existing workflows. This does not always require the API — many integration points are simpler.
Slack and Communication Tools
Teams frequently ask: 'can Claude be in our Slack?' The claude.ai interface itself does not have a native Slack app. Integration options are:
Manual copy-paste workflow:
The simplest 'integration'. Members draft in Claude, paste to Slack. No engineering required; works immediately. High friction kills adoption for quick questions.
Anthropic API + Slack app:

Engineering builds a Slack bot that routes messages to the API. Full flexibility; requires maintenance. Common for customer-facing support bots where Claude's response is validated and formatted before delivery.
Third-party Slack + Claude connectors:
Services like Zapier, Make, or n8n can route Slack messages to the Anthropic API and post responses. Lower engineering cost than building from scratch; less flexible.
Document Workflows
Google Workspace and Microsoft 365 are where most professional writing happens. Claude can be integrated at several points:

Drafting in Claude, editing in Docs/Word: members draft in claude.ai, paste into their document editor. Effective for long-form content where Claude handles structure and the member handles final voice.
Anthropic API + Google Apps Script: custom Docs add-ons that send selected text to Claude for rewrite, summarisation, or review. Engineering effort is moderate (1–2 days for a basic implementation).

Claude for editing prompts: paste a document excerpt into Claude with a specific editing instruction ('rewrite this paragraph to be more direct; target audience is a CFO'). More effective than asking Claude to 'improve' vague content.
Code and Development Workflows
Engineering teams have the highest-value Claude use cases in most organisations. Common patterns:
IDE integration via Claude API:
Custom plugins or VS Code extensions that send code context to Claude for review, explanation, or generation. Requires API access and engineering effort.
CI/CD integration:
Automated PR review using Claude via the API, triggered by GitHub Actions or GitLab CI. Claude comments on PRs with style, logic, and security observations. Human reviewer makes final call.
Shared Code Review Project:

A claude.ai Shared Project with coding standards in knowledge files and a code review instruction. Engineers paste code snippets; Claude reviews against team standards. No API required; works immediately with Team Plan.
Summary

Claude in the Org is a four-layer system: IT controls access and identity, department AI leads configure workspaces and Projects, individual contributors use Claude in their daily workflows, and engineering integrates Claude into automated systems via the API. Each layer has distinct responsibilities and decisions. Successful deployment requires all four layers to function — access without configuration, or configuration without adoption, produces waste. A structured pilot, clear policy, and designated AI leads per team are the three non-technical factors that most predict whether an organisation's Claude deployment generates lasting value.

The output of this review is a list of 3–5 improvements: new Projects to create, instruction updates, files to refresh, and use cases to avoid. Doing this quarterly prevents Projects from becoming stale and keeps adoption growing.

Without coordination, departments develop siloed expertise and repeat each other's mistakes. With a monthly sync, a support team's discovery that Claude handles a particular complaint pattern well propagates to other teams in the same call.
